lines.text: SEGMENT 5 — Examples of broken brakes
Now the other side.
The tumor suppressors.
The brakes that, when they fail, fail by being absent.
If oncogenes have a star, tumor suppressors have an emperor, and p53 is the emperor.
It's nicknamed "the guardian of the genome," and the nickname is well earned.
Here's its job.
Imagine a factory inspector who walks the assembly line constantly.
Whenever a cell senses trouble — DNA damage, oxygen starvation, conflicting growth signals, anything off — p53 levels rise in the nucleus.
And p53 then has to make a decision.
If the damage is light, p53 pauses the assembly line — stops the cell from dividing — and calls in the repair crews to fix the DNA.
If the damage is too severe to repair, p53 makes a more drastic call: it orders the cell to commit suicide.
It triggers apoptosis.
The cell dismantles itself before it can pass damaged DNA to its descendants.
You can see why losing p53 is catastrophic.
With p53 gone, damaged cells keep dividing, mutations accumulate unchecked, and the brakes against malignancy come off all at once.
About half of all human cancers have mutated p53.
Half.
It is the single most commonly mutated gene in cancer.
And it's a tumor suppressor — both copies have to be knocked out — which is exactly the pattern Knudson predicted.
People have spent decades trying to make a drug that restores p53 function, and it's brutally hard.
You can't easily replace a missing protein with a small molecule.

There are experimental approaches — drugs that try to restore mutant p53 to its normal shape, or that exploit the consequences of p53 loss rather than the loss itself — but as of right now, p53 is largely an explanation, not a target.
Most cancers carry this broken brake, and we can't, directly, fix it.
RB.
Retinoblastoma protein, the one from Knudson's hypothesis.
RB sits at a specific checkpoint in the cell cycle — a checkpoint we'll dig into next segment — and asks, essentially, "is now the right time to commit to dividing?
" If the answer is no, RB physically holds back the gas pedal.
When RB gets knocked out, the cell sails past that checkpoint without thinking.
Some childhood cancers have RB mutations from birth, but RB also gets disabled in many adult cancers, sometimes by mutation, sometimes by being chemically silenced.
BRCA1 and BRCA2.
You've probably heard of these because of Angelina Jolie.
BRCA genes are part of the DNA repair machinery — specifically, a flavor of repair called homologous recombination, which fixes nasty double-strand breaks in DNA.

People who inherit one broken copy of BRCA1 or BRCA2 have a substantially elevated lifetime risk of breast and ovarian cancer, because in any cell where the second copy happens to also get knocked out, that cell loses a major DNA repair pathway and starts accumulating mutations rapidly — including, eventually, mutations that push it toward cancer.
Here's the beautiful twist with BRCA.
The broken brake creates a vulnerability we can exploit.
Cells have multiple DNA repair pathways, like a building has multiple fire exits.
BRCA cells have lost one exit.
If we use a drug to block one of the other exits — a class of drugs called PARP inhibitors — normal cells shrug, because they still have their BRCA-driven exit working.
But cancer cells in a BRCA patient, which have already lost the BRCA exit, now have no exits left, and they die.
This trick is called synthetic lethality — two non-lethal hits that together are lethal — and it's one of the few elegant ways we have to exploit the loss of a tumor suppressor.
It's not restoring the brake; it's noticing that the broken brake leaves the car relying entirely on the emergency brake, and then sabotaging that.
PTEN.
PTEN is a brake on a particular signaling highway called the PI3K-AKT pathway, which when active tells cells to grow, survive, and consume nutrients.
PTEN's job is to stand in that highway and slow traffic.
When PTEN gets knocked out — common in many cancers — the highway runs unchecked, and cells get persistent grow-and-survive signals.
PTEN loss is again the absence of a brake; we're back to the same hard problem.
So compare the two segments.
Stuck gas pedals — RAS, EGFR, BCR-ABL — are usually a positive presence you can attack.
Bad protein doing a bad thing; build a drug that obstructs it.
Broken brakes — p53, RB, BRCA, PTEN — are an absence.
Nothing to attack directly.
You either find a clever indirect angle, like synthetic lethality, or you have to make peace with the fact that the brake is gone and design your treatment around what's left.
This asymmetry — gas pedals are druggable, brakes mostly aren't — is one of the deepest reasons modern cancer therapy looks the way it does.
Almost every targeted drug you'll ever hear about is targeting a stuck gas pedal of some kind.
The broken brakes lurk in the background, making everything worse, and most of the time we can only treat around them.
